title: BGP EVPN Route Withdrawal Rate and Flap Storms
app: Custom BGP monitor, `TA-cisco_ios`, Arista eAPI scripted input
spl:
index=network sourcetype="bgp:evpn_events" earliest=-1h
| where match(lower(event_type),"(?i)withdraw|wdr|revoke")
| bin _time span=1m
| stats count as wdr by _time, host, peer_ip
| where wdr > 200
| sort -wdr
| table _time, host, peer_ip, wdr
description: Withdrawal storms shrink effective ECMP sets and extend convergence after link or node events, which shows up as application timeouts and storage path loss. Measuring withdrawal velocity per peer differentiates normal housekeeping from dangerous churn in the EVPN control plane.
value: Withdrawal storms shrink effective ECMP sets and extend convergence after link or node events, which shows up as application timeouts and storage path loss. Measuring withdrawal velocity per peer differentiates normal housekeeping from dangerous churn in the EVPN control plane.
implementation: (1) Stream BGP UPDATE syslog or BMP into `bgp:evpn_events` with normalized `event_type`. (2) Tune per-fabric scale; exclude RR-only peers if needed. (3) Correlate with spine-leaf neighbor state (UC-18.3.14).
visualization: Timechart (withdrawals per minute), Table (worst peers), Single value (peak wdr).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.dest span=1m | sort - count
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
тАв Install and configure the required add-on or app: Custom BGP monitor, `TA-cisco_ios`, Arista eAPI scripted input.
тАв Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=network` `sourcetype="bgp:evpn_events"` with fields `host`, `peer_ip`, `event_type`, `rd`, `prefix`.
тАв For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 тАФ Configure data collection
(1) Stream BGP UPDATE syslog or BMP into `bgp:evpn_events` with normalized `event_type`. (2) Tune per-fabric scale; exclude RR-only peers if needed. (3) Correlate with spine-leaf neighbor state (UC-18.3.14).

Step 2 тАФ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=network sourcetype="bgp:evpn_events" earliest=-1h
| where match(lower(event_type),"(?i)withdraw|wdr|revoke")
| bin _time span=1m
| stats count as wdr by _time, host, peer_ip
| where wdr > 200
| sort -wdr
| table _time, host, peer_ip, wdr
```

Understanding this SPL

**BGP EVPN Route Withdrawal Rate and Flap Storms** тАФ Withdrawal storms shrink effective ECMP sets and extend convergence after link or node events, which shows up as application timeouts and storage path loss. Measuring withdrawal velocity per peer differentiates normal housekeeping from dangerous churn in the EVPN control plane.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=network` `sourcetype="bgp:evpn_events"` with fields `host`, `peer_ip`, `event_type`, `rd`, `prefix`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Custom BGP monitor, `TA-cisco_ios`, Arista eAPI scripted input. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedтАФrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: network; **sourcetype**: bgp:evpn_events. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

тАв Scopes the data: index=network, sourcetype="bgp:evpn_events", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
тАв Filters the current rows with `where match(lower(event_type),"(?i)withdraw|wdr|revoke")` тАФ typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
тАв Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
тАв `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time, host, peer_ip** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
тАв Filters the current rows with `where wdr > 200` тАФ typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
тАв Orders rows with `sort` тАФ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
тАв Pipeline stage (see **BGP EVPN Route Withdrawal Rate and Flap Storms**): table _time, host, peer_ip, wdr

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.dest span=1m | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**BGP EVPN Route Withdrawal Rate and Flap Storms** тАФ Withdrawal storms shrink effective ECMP sets and extend convergence after link or node events, which shows up as application timeouts and storage path loss. Measuring withdrawal velocity per peer differentiates normal housekeeping from dangerous churn in the EVPN control plane.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=network` `sourcetype="bgp:evpn_events"` with fields `host`, `peer_ip`, `event_type`, `rd`, `prefix`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Custom BGP monitor, `TA-cisco_ios`, Arista eAPI scripted input. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedтАФrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

тАв Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` тАФ enable acceleration for that model.
тАв Orders rows with `sort` тАФ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 тАФ Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 тАФ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Timechart (withdrawals per minute), Table (worst peers), Single value (peak wdr).
grandmaExplanation: Withdrawal storms shrink effective ECMP sets and extend convergence after link or node events, which shows up as application timeouts and storage path loss тАФ so traffic problems, routing mistakes, and risky changes surface fast.
hardware: BGP EVPN-capable leaf and spine switches
